questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q:
$\sum_{k=1}^{40}\left(\sqrt{1+\frac{1}{k^2}+\frac{1}{(k+1)^2}}\right)=a+\frac{b}{c}$
where $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}, b < c, \operatorname{gcd}(b, c)=1$, then what is the value of $a+b$ ?
questions.solution:
Let
$\begin{aligned}
t_k & =\sqrt{1+\frac{1}{k^2}+\frac{1}{(k+1)^2}} \\
& =\sqrt{\frac{k^2(k+1)^2+k^2+(k+1)^2}{k^2(k+1)^2}} \\
& =\sqrt{\frac{k^4+2 k^3+3 k^2+2 k+1}{k^2(k+1)^2}}=\sqrt{\frac{\left(k^2+k+1\right)^2}{k^2(k+1)^2}} \\
& =\frac{k^2+k+1}{k(k+1)}=1+\frac{1}{k(k+1)} \\
& =1+\frac{1}{k}-\frac{1}{k+1}
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad S_n=\sum_{k=1}^n t_k=t_1+t_2+\ldots \ldots+t_n \\ & \quad=n+1-\frac{1}{n+1}=(n+1)-\frac{1}{n+1}=n+\frac{n}{n+1} \\ & \quad \sum_{k=1}^{40} \sqrt{1+\frac{1}{k^2}+\frac{1}{(k+1)^2}}=40+\frac{40}{41} \\ & \therefore \quad a=b=40, c=41 \\ & \therefore \quad a+b=80 .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: A group of women working together at the same rate can build a wall in 45 hours. When the work started, all the women did not start working together. They joined the work over a period of time, one by one, at equal intervals. Once at work, each one stayed till the work was complete. If the first woman worked 5 times as many hours as the last woman, for how many hours did the first woman work?
questions.solution:
Let $n$ women complete the work in 45 hr .
Then 1 woman can complete the work in $45 n \mathrm{hr}$.
$\therefore$ Each woman's work of $1 \mathrm{hr}=\frac{1}{45 n}$.
According to given question let each woman join the work after $d$ hrs. of previous women started the work.
Now, total work is finished in $T$ hours after the work started by first woman.
Time for which the first woman work $=T \mathrm{hr}$.
Time for which the $n^{\text {th }}$ women work $=\{T-(n-1) d\} \mathrm{hr}$.
A.T.Q : $5\{T-(n-1) d\}=T$
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \frac{4 T}{5}=(n-1) d \\
& \text { and } \frac{T}{45 n}+\frac{T-d}{45 n}+\frac{T-2 d}{45 n}+\ldots+\frac{T-(n-1) d}{45 n}=1 \\
& \Rightarrow n T-\frac{n(n-1)}{2} d=45 n \\
& \Rightarrow 2 T-(n-1) d=90 \\
& \Rightarrow 2 T-\frac{4 T}{5}=90 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{6 T}{5}=90 \\
& \therefore T=\frac{90 \times 5}{6}=75
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Total time for which first women work $=75 \mathrm{hrs}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Consider the set T of all triangles whose sides are distinct prime numbers which are also in arithmetic progression. Let $\Delta \in \mathrm{T}$ be the triangle with the least perimeter. If $\mathrm{a}^{\circ}$ is the largest angle of $\Delta$ and if $L$ is its perimeter, determine the value of $\frac{a}{L}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q:
Let $m$ be the smallest positive integer such that $m^2+(m+1)^2+\ldots+(m+10)^2$ is the square of a positive integer
$n$. Find $m+n$.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{r=1}^{11}(m+r-1)^2 & =11 m^2+\left(1^2+2^2+3^2+\ldots+10^2\right)+2 m(1+2+3+\ldots+10) \\
& =11\left(m^2+10 m+35\right) \\
& =11\left((m+5)^2+10\right)
\end{aligned}$

The least possible value of $m=18$
The required sum $=11\left(23^2+10\right)$
$\begin{aligned}
& =11 \times 11 \times 49 \\
& =77^2
\end{aligned}$

Then, $m=18$ and $n=77$
$\therefore \quad m+n=95$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Starting with a positive integer $M$ written on the board, Alice plays the following game: in each move, if $x$ is the number on the board, she replaces it with $3 x+2$. Similarly, starting with a positive integer $N$ written on the board, Bob plays the following game: in each move, if $x$ is the number on the board, he replaces it with $2 x+27$. Given that Alice and Bob reach the same number after playing 4 moves each, find the smallest value of $M+N$.
questions.solution:
In 4 steps Alice will write
$(3(3(3(3 M+2)+2)+2)+2)=\alpha$
and Bob will write
$(2(2(2(2 N+27)+27)+27)+27)=\beta$

As $\alpha=\beta$, we get
$\begin{array}{ll} 
& 81 M=16 N+325 \\
\therefore \quad & M_{\min }=5 \text { and } N_{\min }=5 \\
& (M+N)_{\min }=10
\end{array}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: The sequence $\left\langle a_n\right\rangle_{n \geq 0}$ is defined by $a_0=1, a_1=-4$ and $a_{n+2}=-4 a_{n+1}-7 a_n$, for $n \geq 0$. Find the number of positive integer divisors of $a_{50}^2-a_{49} a_{51}$.
questions.solution:
The sequence $\left\{a_n\right\}, n \geq 0$ and $a_0=1, a_1=-4$ and given that $a_{n+2}=-4 a_{n+1}-7 a_n$......(1)
Now,
$\begin{aligned} & a_n^2-a_{n+1} \cdot a_{n-1}=a_n^2-\left(4 a_n-7 a_{n-1}\right) a_{n-1} \\ & =a_n^2+4 a_n \cdot a_{n-1}+7 a_{n-1}^2 \\ & =-a_n\left(7 a_{n-2}\right)+7 a_{n-1}^2 \\ & =7\left(a_{n-1}^2-a_n \cdot a_{n-2}\right) \\ & =7^2\left(a_{n-1}^2-a_{n-1} \cdot a_{n-3}\right)\end{aligned}$
.................................
$\begin{array}{ll}
\therefore & a_n^2-a_{n+1} a_{n-1}=7^{n-1}\left(a_1^2-a_0 a_2\right)=7^{n-1}(16-1 \cdot 9)=7^n \\
\therefore & a_{50}^2-a_{49} \cdot a_{51}=7^{50}
\end{array}$
$\therefore \quad$ Number of positive integer divisors of $7^{50}=50+1=51$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: If $\sum_{k=1}^N \frac{2 k+1}{\left(k^2+k\right)^2}=0.9999$ then determine the value of $N$.

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let the sum $\sum_{n=1}^9 \frac{1}{n(n+1)(n+2)}$ written in its lowest terms be $\frac{p}{q}$. Find the value of $q-p$.

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{u}, \mathrm{v}, \mathrm{w}$ be real numbers in geometric progression such that $\mathrm{u}>\mathrm{v}>\mathrm{w}$. Suppose $\mathrm{u}^{40}=\mathrm{v}^{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{w}^{60}$. Find the value of $n$.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & u=a \\ & v=a r \\ & w=a r^2 \\ & a^{40}=(a r)^n=\left(a r^2\right)^{60} \\ & a^{20}=r^{-120} \Rightarrow a=r^{-6} \\ & r^{-240}=r^{-5 n} \Rightarrow \quad 5 n=240 \\ & n=\frac{240}{5}=48\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Five distinct 2-digit numbers are in a geometric progression. Find the middle term.
questions.solution:
Let the numbers be $\quad a \quad$ ar $\quad a r^2 \quad a r^3 \quad a r^4$ since all are 2 digit number $r=\frac{2}{3}$ or $\frac{3}{2}$ (as fourth power of integers greater than 3 are 3 digit numbers)
Hence the five numbers are $(16,24,36,54,81)$
Hence middle term is 36 .

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Integers $1,2,3 \ldots \ldots . \mathrm{n}$ where $\mathrm{n}>2$, are written on a board. Two numbers $\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{k}$ such that $1 < \mathrm{m} < \mathrm{n} .1 < \mathrm{k} < \mathrm{n}$ are removed and the average of the remaining numbers is found to be 17 . What is the maximum sum of the two removed numbers?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\frac{n(n+1)}{2}-(2 n-1)}{n-2} < 17 < \frac{\frac{n(n+1)}{2}-3}{n-2} \\ & \frac{n^2+n-4 n+2}{2(n-2)} < 17 < \frac{n^2+n-6}{2(n-2)} \\ & \frac{n^2+3 n+2}{2(n-2)} < 17 < \frac{(n+3)(n-2)}{2(n-2)} \\ & \frac{n-1}{2} < 17 < \frac{n+3}{2} \\ & n < 35 \text { and } n>31 \\ & n=32,33,34 \\ & \text { case- } 1, n=32\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\frac{n(n+1)}{2}-p}{(n-2)}=17 \Rightarrow \frac{n(n+1)}{2}-17(n-2)=p \\ & p=18 \\ & \text { case- } 2, n=33 \Rightarrow p=34 \\ & \text { case- } 3, n=34 \Rightarrow p=51 \\ & \text { Maximum sum }=51\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: A finite set $M$ of positive integers consists of distinct perfect squares and the number 92 . The average of the numbers in $M$ is 85 . If we remove 92 from $M$, the average drops to 84 . If $N^2$ is the largest possible square in $M$, what is the value of $N$ ?

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Suppose x is a positive real number such that $\{\mathrm{x}\} .[\mathrm{x}]$ and x are in the geometric progression. Find the least positive integer $n$ such that $x^n>100$. (Here $[x]$ denotes the integer part of $x$ and $\{x\}=x-[x])$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & {[x]^2=x\{x\}} \\ & \{x\}=a \\ & {[x]=a r} \\ & x=a r^2 \\ & a+a r=a r^2 \\ & r^2-r-1=0 \\ & r=\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2} \Rightarrow r=\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \\ & a r=I \\ & a=\frac{2 I}{(1+\sqrt{5})} \\ & a=\frac{I(\sqrt{5}-1)}{2}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & 0 < a < 1 \\ & 0 < \frac{I(\sqrt{5}-1)}{2} < 1 \\ & 0 < I < \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}-1} \\ & 0 < I < \frac{\sqrt{5}+1}{2} \\ & I=1\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & a r=1 \Rightarrow a=\frac{2}{1+\sqrt{5}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2} \\ & x=a r^2=r=\frac{\sqrt{5}+1}{2} \\ & \left(\frac{\sqrt{5}+1}{2}\right)^N>100 \Rightarrow N \log _{10}\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}+1}{2}\right)>2 \\ & N>9.5 \Rightarrow N_{\min }=10\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Each of the numbers $\mathrm{x}_1, \mathrm{x}_2, \ldots \ldots \ldots . \mathrm{x}_{101}$ is $\pm 1$. What is the smallest positive value of $\sum_{1 \leq i \leq j \leq 101} x_i x_j$ ?
questions.solution:
Let $S=\sum_{1 \leq i \leq j \leq 10} x_i x_j$
We have
$\begin{aligned}
& \left(x_1+x_2+x_3+\ldots \ldots x_{101}\right)^2=x_1^2+x_2^2+x_3^2+\ldots \ldots . .+x_{101}^2+2 S \\
& \Rightarrow 2 S=\left(\sum_{i=1}^{101} x_i\right)^2-\sum_{i=1}^{101} x_i^2
\end{aligned}$

Since we have $x_i= \pm 1$, So $x_i^2=1$
So $2 S=\left(\sum_{i=1}^{101} x_i\right)^2-101$
Since $\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{101} \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}$ will be an integer
So $\left(\sum_{i=1}^{101} x_i\right)^2$ will be a perfect square.
For smallest positive $S:\left(\sum_{i=1}^{101} x_i\right)^2$ must be the smallest perfect square greater than 101 .
So $\left(\sum_{i=1}^{101} x_i\right)^2=121$
$\Rightarrow \sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{101} \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}=11 \text { or }-11$

We can verify that the desired sum can be achieved by putting $45 x_i$ 's to be -1 and $56 x_i$ 's to be 1 So, 2S = 121-101=20
$\Rightarrow S=10$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q:
A natural number $\mathrm{k}>1$ is called good if there exist natural numbers
$a_1 < a_2 < \ldots \ldots \ldots < a_k$
such that $\quad \frac{1}{\sqrt{a_1}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{a_2}}+\ldots \ldots+\frac{1}{\sqrt{a_k}}=1$
Let $f(n)$ be the sum of the first $n$ good numbers, $n \geq 1$. Find the sum of all values of $n$ for which $f(n+5) / f(n)$ is an integer.
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{a}_1=\mathrm{A}_1^2, \mathrm{a}_2=\mathrm{A}_2^2 \cdots \cdots \cdots \cdots \cdot \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{k}}^2$
we have to check if it is possible for distinct natural number $\mathrm{A}_1, \mathrm{~A}_2$. $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{k}}$ to satisfy,
$\frac{1}{A_1}+\frac{1}{A_2}+\ldots \ldots . \frac{1}{A_k}=1$

For $k=2$; it is obvious that there do not exist distinct $A_1, A_2$, such that $\frac{1}{A_1}+\frac{1}{A_2}=1 \Rightarrow 2$ is not a good number.
For $\mathrm{k}=3$; we have $\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{6}=1 \Rightarrow 3$ is a good number.
$\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{6}\right)=1 \Rightarrow 4$ is a good number.
$k$ will be a good numbers for all $k \geq 3$
$\begin{aligned}
& f(n)=3+4+\ldots n \text { terms }=\frac{n(n+5)}{2} ; f(n+5)=\frac{(n+5)(n+10)}{2} \\
& \frac{f(n+5)}{f(n)}=\frac{n+10}{n}=1+\frac{10}{n}
\end{aligned}$

Which will be integer for $\mathrm{n}=1,2,5$ and 10
$\text { sum }=1+2+5+10=18$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q:
Let $a_1=24$ and form the sequence $a_n, n \geq 2$ by $a_n=100 a_{n-1}+134$. The first few terms are
$24,2534,253534,25353534, \ldots \ldots \ldots$

What is the least value of n for which $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}$ is divisible by 99 ?
questions.solution:
$a_1=24 \& a_n=100 . a_{n-1}+134$

First few terms :
$\begin{aligned}
& a_1=24 \\
& a_2=2534
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & a_3=253534 \\ & a_4=25353534 \\ & \therefore a_n=2 \underbrace{535353 \ldots \ldots . .534}_{(n-1) \text { Times }^{\prime} 53^{\prime}}\end{aligned}$

Now, an $\rightarrow$ divisible by $99 \Rightarrow$ by $9 \& 11$ both
Sum of digits $=6+8(n-1)$
To be divisible by 9 ,
$n=7,16,25,34,43,52,61,70,79,88, \ldots .$
$\mathrm{a}_7=2 \underbrace{535353535353}_{6 \text { Times }{ }^{\prime} 53^{\prime}} 4$
But $\mathrm{a}_7 \rightarrow$ Not divisible by 11 .
$\mathrm{a}_{16}=2 \underbrace{5353535353 \ldots . .53}_{15 \text { Times'53' }} 4$
Similarly, $\mathrm{a}_{16} \rightarrow$ Not divisible by 11 .
Now, $\mathrm{n}=88$
$\mathrm{a}_{88}=2 \underbrace{5353 \ldots \ldots 53}_{87 \text { Times }{ }^{\prime} 53^{\prime}} 4$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Divisibility by } \begin{aligned} 11 & \rightarrow|(2+3+3 \ldots . .)-(5+5+\ldots .4)| \\ & =|263-439| \\ & =176\end{aligned} \\ & \therefore \text { Least } n=88\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: A natural number $n$ is said to be good if $n$ is the sum of $r$ consecutive positive integers, for some $r \geq 2$. Find the number of good numbers in the set $\{1,2, \ldots \ldots, 100)$.
questions.solution:
All odd numbers are good because,
$2 K+1=K+(K+1)$

All even numbers which have an odd factor greater than 1 are also good because,
$\begin{array}{r}
2^n \cdot(2 K+1)=K+\underbrace{(K \pm 1)+(K \pm 2)+\ldots+\left(K \pm\left(2^n-1\right)\right)}_{\left(2^n-1\right) \text { terms }} \\
+\left(K+2^n\right)
\end{array}$

So, only $2^n$ are the numbers which are not good.
Numbers which are not good are $2^0, 2^1, 2^2, 2^3, 2^4$, $2^5$ and $2^6$.

Remaining 93 numbers are all good.

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q:
What is the largest positive integer n such that
$\frac{a^2}{\frac{b}{29}+\frac{c}{31}}+\frac{b^2}{\frac{c}{29}+\frac{a}{31}}+\frac{c^2}{\frac{a}{29}+\frac{b}{31}} \geq n(a+b+c)$ holds for all positive real numbers a,b,c.

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: What is the value of $\sum_{\substack{1 \leq i < j \leq 10 \\ i+j=\text { odd }}}(i+j)-\sum_{\substack{1 \leq i < j \leq 10 \\ i+j=\text { even }}}(i+j)$ ?
questions.solution: Sum of odd $=\underbrace{3+5+7+9+\ldots \ldots+19}_9+\underbrace{5+7+\ldots \ldots+17}_7+\underbrace{7+9+\ldots \ldots \ldots+15}_5+\underbrace{9+11+13}_3+11$ Sum of even $=\underbrace{4+6+\ldots \ldots+18}_8+\underbrace{6+8+\ldots \ldots+16}_6+\underbrace{8+10+12+14}_4+\underbrace{10+12}_2$ $\text { Difference }=(-1-1-1 \ldots \ldots \ldots .8 \text { times })+19+(-1-1-1 \ldots \ldots \ldots .6 \text { times })+17+(-1-1-1-1)+15+(-1-1)+13+11$ $=-8-6-4-2+19+17+15+13+11=75-20=55$
